title: How Trump Helped China Make America’s Cheapest EV
gist: Killing the US EV tax credit paradoxically freed automakers like Slate to adopt cheaper Chinese-perfected LFP battery chemistry, deepening US dependence on Chinese battery technology even as most new domestic LFP output is being diverted from EVs to stationary energy storage.

themes.theme: Slate and the LFP cost play

insights.claim: Michigan upstart Slate's new electric truck starts just below $25,000 versus a ~$55,000 average EV price, reaching that floor partly by using a cheaper lithium iron phosphate (LFP) battery pack instead of nickel manganese cobalt (NMC).
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: A sub-$25k EV built on LFP shows the chemistry, not just stripped features, is the lever for hitting affordability targets that mass-market EV adoption requires.
insights.quote: the small, modular offering from Michigan-based automaking upstart Slate costs just below $25,000 for its base model
insights.locator: That makes America’s newest and cheapest electric truck

insights.claim: Slate will source LFP batteries from Gotion, a US-based subsidiary of a Chinese firm, manufactured in Illinois, and its truck now claims 205 miles of range, up from the originally promised 150.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Even US-assembled LFP packs carry Chinese corporate parentage, illustrating that domestic 'onshoring' often still routes through Chinese-owned technology.
insights.quote: Slate is planning to use batteries made by Gotion, a US-based subsidiary of a Chinese firm.
insights.locator: After reviewing its options, Slate is planning

themes.theme: Policy reversal and its second-order effects

insights.claim: The 2022 climate law's $7,500 EV tax credit required US-assembled batteries and discouraged materials from 'foreign entities of concern' including China, which made LFP batteries problematic to use and steered manufacturers away from the chemistry.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: The prior credit's content rules were the binding constraint suppressing US LFP adoption, showing how subsidy design can steer chemistry choices.
insights.locator: The reason was simple: In 2022, Congress passed

insights.claim: Last summer the GOP-led Congress killed the EV tax credit to fulfill Trump's promise to 'end the electric vehicle mandate,' removing the foreign-content penalty and opening the door for Slate and others to reconsider Chinese-linked LFP batteries.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: The central irony: a policy meant to reduce Chinese reliance, once repealed, removed the incentive to avoid Chinese battery tech, deepening dependence.
insights.quote: they no longer have to worry about the foreign content of their EV batteries for fear of losing the tax credit.
insights.locator: Now automakers have to deal with

insights.claim: BloombergNEF predicted US EV sales will fall 19 percent this year due to the tax-credit repeal and automakers' resulting decisions to cut EV output.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: A projected ~19% US EV sales decline reprices demand assumptions for automakers and battery suppliers exposed to the US market.
insights.quote: US sales will fall by 19 percent this year because of the policy change
insights.locator: Research firm BloombergNEF predicted earlier this month

insights.claim: 97.8 percent of LFP cathode production takes place in China, and nearly 85 percent of all cathode production overall, per Benchmark Mineral Intelligence.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Near-total Chinese control of LFP cathodes is a hard supply-chain chokepoint that any US LFP strategy must confront, favoring firms building domestic capacity.
insights.quote: Today, 97.8 percent of LFP cathode production takes place in China, according to figures from Benchmark Mineral Intelligence
insights.locator: Since then, Chinese EV giants including BYD and CATL

insights.claim: LFP battery applications were discovered by US scientists in the 1960s, but Chinese manufacturers like BYD and CATL perfected the chemistry by accepting its range limits in exchange for lower cost and stability, building the full mine-to-cathode supply chain.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: The classic US-invents/China-commercializes pattern; the moat is manufacturing scale and supply-chain integration, not the underlying IP.
insights.quote: Chinese manufacturers, though, decided they were willing to exchange LFP chemistry’s range issues for its promise to lower costs and improve stability.
insights.locator: Those rules made it problematic to use LFP batteries

themes.theme: US LFP buildout diverted to storage

insights.claim: Multiple mainstream models already use LFP—standard-range Tesla Model 3/Y (250 miles), standard Mustang Mach-E (250 miles), and the updated Chevrolet Bolt (260 miles)—with GM temporarily importing Bolt LFP cells from China while it stands up domestic lines.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: LFP is already mainstream in affordable US trims, so demand is real; the constraint is domestic supply, keeping near-term imports flowing from China.
insights.locator: Other automakers have turned to LFP batteries

insights.claim: LG Energy Solution expects to produce 50 gigawatt-hours of LFP capacity in North America this year, more than tripling last year's output, with five of its eight North American plants building LFP.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Rapid domestic LFP capacity growth exists but remains dwarfed by China; LGES is a key name to watch on US battery localization.
insights.quote: It expects to produce 50 gigawatt-hours of LFP capacity this year, more than tripling last year’s output.
insights.locator: Five of the Korean firm LG Energy Solutions’

insights.claim: Most US-made LFP won't go into EVs but into energy storage systems, with LGES, GM, Ford and Samsung all converting EV-intended LFP lines to stationary storage to maintain cash flow amid the EV sales dip.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: The demand pivot from EVs to grid storage reroutes battery capex toward the energy-storage market—a nearer-term revenue pool for cell makers than a soft US EV market.
insights.quote: In the past half-year alone, LGES, GM, Ford, and Samsung have all said that they’re converting LFP battery production lines meant for EVs to instead churn out energy storage
insights.locator: For now though, most of LGES’s LFP batteries

insights.claim: LFP is arguably a better fit for stationary storage than cars because it is stable, safe, long-lasting, and its heavier weight matters less.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: The chemistry's physical properties structurally favor grid storage, suggesting the storage-over-EV pivot is durable, not just a cyclical stopgap.
insights.quote: LFPs are arguably a better fit for stationary storage than they are for cars because they’re stable, safe, and long-lasting, plus their heavier weight doesn’t matter as much.
insights.locator: They’ll instead be used for energy storage systems

insights.claim: LG Energy Solution North America president Bob Lee argues the most important factor for US battery manufacturers to thrive is not a return of tax credits but policy stability that doesn't swing wildly between administrations.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Capital allocators can invest around unfavorable-but-stable policy, so regulatory volatility, not subsidy level, is the true deterrent to US battery investment.
insights.quote: Even if you have policies that are not so friendly, if it's set and if it's somehow going to be stable, then you can still invest your way around it and plan
insights.locator: The most important element to ensure US manufacturers thrive

insights.claim: The US still lacks the supporting manufacturing ecosystem—even simple stamping and metal parts must be imported—and needs billions more in facilities plus skilled local workforces to build a self-sufficient LFP supply chain.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Domestic LFP independence is a multi-year, capital-heavy project; near term, Chinese technology stays under the hood of US EVs even without direct Chinese vehicle imports.
insights.quote: Sometimes even simple metal parts, stamping parts—you don't have all the manufacturing where we are, so we have to import these things
insights.locator: But Lee says the US has plenty of work to do
